coredata.dc:title: Performance assessment of carbon dots based nano cutting fluids in improving the machining characteristics of additively manufactured 316L stainless steel
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ross, Nimel Sworna
Gupta, Munish Kumar
Mashinini, Peter Madindwa
Ananth, M. Belsam Jeba
Królczyk, Grzegorz M.
coredata.dc:description: The advantages of additive manufacturing (AM) over conventional manufacturing techniques, such as the ability to build parts with complicated geometry, have led to a rise in its applications in recent years. However, the mechanical and tribological qualities of an additively manufactured (AMed) parts are distinct from those of a conventionally made components because of the layer-by-layer nature of AM process. However, there is a need to machine these complex geometries of AMed components to achieve the desired final dimension and surface finish. Therefore, this study investigates the impact of four environmental regimes (dry, flood, MQL, and N-MQL) on the metal cutting of AMed 316L stainless steel specimen, with a focus on the efficacy of N-MQL lubrication containing rice bran oil + carbon dots (CDs) particles. Responses measured include cutting temperature, surface roughness (Ra), tool wear, microstructure, and micro-hardness. Outcomes show that N-MQL outperforms other cutting regimes, offering enhanced machining performance and surface quality, making it a crucial choice for AMed specimens.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Additive manufacturing
Machining
Cooling conditions
Tribology
Microstructure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fabrication of LPBF AISI 316L SS, (b) Experimental setup of milling.
Variation of Vb at (a) 45 m/min and (b) 75/min under distinct coolants.
Shear zones in metal cutting with tool wear mechanism.
(a–d) Wear mechanism under distinct cooling regimes with SEM/EDX.
Variation of cutting temperature at (a) 45 m/min and (b) 75/min under distinct coolants.
Variation of Ra at (a) 45 m/min and (b) 75/min under distinct coolants.
3D topography (left) and SEM images (right) under distinct cooling regimes at a Vc of 75 m/min.
Variation of hardness at a Vc = 75 m/min under distinct coolants.
Variation of microstructure at a Vc = 75/min under distinct coolants.

head.ce:title: Performance assessment of carbon dots based nano cutting fluids in improving the machining characteristics of additively manufactured 316L stainless steel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology Kattankulathur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechatronics Engineering, SRM Institute of Science and Technology Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu. 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The advantages of additive manufacturing (AM) over conventional manufacturing techniques, such as the ability to build parts with complicated geometry, have led to a rise in its applications in recent years. However, the mechanical and tribological qualities of an additively manufactured (AMed) parts are distinct from those of a conventionally made components because of the layer-by-layer nature of AM process. However, there is a need to machine these complex geometries of AMed components to achieve the desired final dimension and surface finish. Therefore, this study investigates the impact of four environmental regimes (dry, flood, MQL, and N-MQL) on the metal cutting of AMed 316L stainless steel specimen, with a focus on the efficacy of N-MQL lubrication containing rice bran oil + carbon dots (CDs) particles. Responses measured include cutting temperature, surface roughness (Ra), tool wear, microstructure, and micro-hardness. Outcomes show that N-MQL outperforms other cutting regimes, offering enhanced machining performance and surface quality, making it a crucial choice for AMed specimens.

ce:section.ce:para: The AM process has received a lot of consideration in the field of materials science and processing methods [1,2] due to its prospective advantages such as more effective customization and optimization of geometrically complicated components [3]. AMed metal parts can be put mainly into 2 classes based on the energy source employed in the depositing procedure: laser-based and electron beam-based [4,5]. Ti alloys, steels, Ni alloys, Al alloys, and other engineered structural metals are so far been processed extensively using AM techniques [6]. Stainless steels (SSs), owning ductility and high strength as well as high resistance to corrosion, served as vital structural material in distinct environments [7]. There has been a wide range of research on AM techniques for 316L austenitic SS. Concerning mechanical qualities, laser-based AM processes for 316L excels beyond that of traditional 316L steels, particularly in terms of yield stress, ultimate stress, and elongation of more than 50 % [8,9]. While advances in technology have made it possible to manufacture near-net-shape components, some functional surfaces may still require semi-finishing or finishing to provide the necessary geometrical tolerances [10].

Machining methodologies are renowned for their adeptness in generating suitably functional surfaces [11,12]. Consequently, it becomes apparent that the application of machining techniques extends to the refinement of AMed components. Nevertheless, it is crucial to acknowledge that the attributes inherent to LPBF materials markedly deviate from those of cast or wrought counterparts, primarily owing to the pronounced temperature differentials experienced within the material during the LPBF process [13]. The distinct procedural regimes inherent to LPBF yield a distinctive, anisotropic, and finely granulated microstructure, which demonstrably enhances strength and toughness properties [14]. The distinctive regimes underpinning the LPBF process precipitate, anisotropic material characteristics, considered by substantial and highly non-uniform residual stresses [15]. As an outcome, the machinability of components produced through the LPBF technique could substantially diverge from their wrought or cast metal counterparts, despite the parity in their chemical compositions. The wrought sample peak at the 2Ɵ of 43.57° is less intense than the as-built SLM made peak with 111 orientation. The as-built SLM produced and the wrought specimens with 111 orientation differ by 170% [16]. Lizzul et al. [17] conducted an examination with tool wear during the milling of parts produced through LPBF using four distinct build orientations. The tool wear was assessed indirectly through meticulous analysis of chip morphology and the quality of the machined faces. This study showcases a gradual reduction in tool lifespan of up to 40 %, as the transition is made from horizontally manufactured samples to vertically manufactured ones. Due to the high cutting power and machining heat used in traditional machining, tool life and machining quality get diminished. Mineral oil-derived cutting fluids (CFs) have historically been the predominant agents utilized to lubricate the cutting areas over the past few decades [18]. Furthermore, with the advent of the sustainable development concept and a focus on more environmentally friendly manufacturing practices, it has become increasingly concerned about the possible threat that CFs represent to both the ecosystem and human health. A preceding investigation underscored the risk to operator well-being posed by the substantial quantity of CF employed in machining operation. The integration of metal fragments and procedural impurities in the CF exacerbates the ecological harm inflicted. Remarkably, statistical data reveals that the formulation, preservation, and post-treatment of CFs contribute to 10–17 % of the overall expenditure within the manufacturing realm [19]. It is now necessary to achieve sustainable machining while maintaining or improving machining performance since doing so offers several economic, environmental, and social benefits. MQL machining is one such eco-friendly process that directly sprays CF into the cutting zone. The MQL process, often known as “near-dry machining,” atomizes the CF (50–500 ml/h) using a specifically created supply system [20]. Bedi et al. [21] investigated the potential of coconut oil with rice bran oil (RBO) in the context of MQL machining of AISI 304 SS. It is noted that MQL with RBO outperforms coconut oil. Attrition, abrasion, adhesion, chip burning and flaking dominate the tool wear in dry cutting. MQL environment has minimal attrition wear and no pitting. Ramoni et al. [22] studied the effect of dry, flood, and MQL cutting environments in the milling of LPBF-produced samples with a PVD-TiAlN coated cutter. MQL creates a thin coating at the point where the tool contacts the material, significantly reducing tool wear. Additionally, penetrability is another factor that contributes to the lowering of flank wear. The highest flank wear is obtained under dry environment.

Nanofluids (NFs), a novel advancement in cooling and lubrication (C/L) techniques, involve the infusion of nano-sized additives (less than 100 nm) into the cutting oil [23]. These nanoparticles (NPs), when introduced to the oil, enhance various fundamental characteristics of the base fluid. This includes improvements in thermal conductivity, wettability, viscosity, and tribological attributes. As a result of these enhancements, significant transformations occur in key performance metrics. These include aiding heat dissipation in the cutting area, decreasing friction, improving surface quality, and elongating of tool life. Researchers have directed their attention towards the utilization of NPs, including Al2O3, CuO, Fe2O3, and ZnO, to investigate their impact on cutting performance attributes. Notably, substantial advancements have been unveiled, particularly in the mitigation of tool life [24]. The variables that affect the machining performance are presented in Fig. 1 .

In the existing body of knowledge, there appears to be a notable absence of studies exploring the combination of CDs + RBO. This apparent gap in research has sparked a keen interest in investigating the potential advantages that might arise from employing CDs, particularly when suspended in RBO, as a method for enhancing machining processes. Specifically, this research delves into the LPBF printed AISI 316L SS, a material known for its widespread industrial applications. One of the pivotal metrics in evaluating the effectiveness of this approach pertains to tool wear. Additionally, an analysis of the cutting temperature, surface topography, hardness, and microstructure with EBSD resulting from this novel machining approach is conducted.

The 316L SS samples, measuring 70 × 50 × 10 mm, were fabricated utilizing the LPBF process, as depicted in Fig. 2 (a). To perform AM, the SLM 280 machine was utilized, featuring a ytterbium fiber laser operating at a speed of 10 m/s. The LPBF process employed spherical metal powder within the size ranges of 30–60 μm. A LT of 30 μm was employed to produce the specimen, and each layer was melted in a straight line through laser scanning within an argon environment. Process parameters consisted are, a laser power of 40 W, and 100 μm parallel scanning. The print orientation is horizontal. Prior to machining, the specimens exhibited an initial Ra value of 0.00725 mm, which is considered inadequate for numerous applications as a reason of its meager surface quality.

The milling process was conducted using the CVM COSMOS 850 machine presented in Fig. 2(b). A WC carbide insert with a TiAlN coating and the appropriate tool holder was selected to cut the LPBF-316L SS. The material is commonly used in various industries such as aerospace, medical implants, jigs, fixtures, and molds. For this investigation on machinability, a workpiece (W/p) measuring 70 × 50 × 10 mm was used. Different cooling methods, including dry, flood, MQL, and N-MQL environments were employed to analyze their impact on the surface behavior. The chemical elements of the LPBF-316L SS utilized in this investigation is the same composition used by Danish et al. [25]. Table 1 presents the experimental details used in this current study. The cutting parameters were chosen based on the manufacturer's preference and previous research. The cutting speed and feed rate considered in this research work are 45–75 m/min and 0.08–0.10 mm/rev, respectively. The depth of cut is constant for all the trials, 0.75 mm.

Dry machining means no CFs or lubricants. Dry cutting eliminates coolant maintenance, fluid supply systems and associated equipment's. This simplifies setup and maintenance costs. The flood cooling regime involves continuously supplying and circulating coolant across the cutting area during machining. The CF floods to the cutting region, aiding the machining process. Flood coolant reduces friction and cutting heat by lubricating the chip-tool-W/p contact area. RBO, a natural and biodegradable oil, is a greener alternative to synthetic coolants. Sustainable and low-impact applications may benefit from it. The viscosity of RBO is 0.0398 Pa.s at 38 °C [26]. A tiny amount of coolant and pressurized air were given to the machining area during MQL. A 3 mm diameter nozzle with a flow rate of 70 ml/h and a minimum pressure of 5 bar is used to supply the coolant.

The two-step technique was implemented for the preparation of NFs due to its numerous advantages. Initially, CDs were synthesized from Hibiscus sabdariffa through green synthesis using the hydrothermal method. With 150 ml of water, fresh leaves were dressed, pulverized, and extracted. The resultant extract was then heated to 160 °C for 8 h in an autoclave. The liquid was cooled, centrifuged to eliminate bigger debris, and then freezed-dried to create CDs. The NFs (CDs + RBO) were developed by adding CDs in varying wt.% from 0.0 to 1.25% to RBO. A surfactant, cetrimonium bromide, was used in this study at a rate of 0.02 wt.%. To guarantee the complete homogeneity, the green-synthesized CDs and RBO were mixed, sonicated for around 30 min using a Qsonica Sonicator, and then magnetically stirred for about 1 h. At 1 wt.% of CDs with RBO, the wettability is higher, the contact angle is lower and provides increased viscosity, the similar result was obtained by Ross et al. [27]. This combination was used for machining with the N-MQL cutting strategy.

The cutting temperature was monitored using a Fluke temperature measurement device. The Fluke device was placed close to the cutting area to obtain real-time temperature readings during the cutting process. The trial was done two times and the average was taken as the cutting temperature. Ra was measured using the SE 3500 Surface Profilometer. Ra was measured along a specific distance (cut-off length 0.8 mm) to ensure an accurate representation of the surface texture. Ra (average roughness) value was determined, as it is a widely used parameter to characterize the fine irregularities on the surface, providing essential information about its quality and functionality. The Ra measurement was taken in five different places on the machined face and the average value was taken. Flank wear (Vb) was evaluated using the video measuring device (VMD) 2010F. The VMD 2010F was allowed for precise and magnified inspection of the cutting tool's Vb. The tool wear results were measured using the Vbmax 0.3 mm criterion. Each sample's surface and wear processes were investigated using a scanning electron microscope (SEM). Additionally, EDX was also performed with the help of the same SEM equipment. EBSD analysis was executed using a SEM equipped with a EBSD detector. EBSD analysis provided critical insights into the microstructure and crystallographic orientation of the machined specimen. Hardness measurement was carried out using a Vickers hardness tester. A specific load (1000 g) was applied to the specimen's surface to determine its hardness.

Metal cutting of additively manufactured AISI 316L SS is a critical process in the manufacturing industry. Vb is an essential parameter that influences tool life and the quality of the machined surface [28]. At Vcs of 45 m/min and 75 m/min, Vb occurs due to the complex interactions amid the cutter and the W/p material. At higher Vc, there is an increased rate of material removal, which principals to higher heat in the cutting area. The elevated temperature accelerates the diffusion of W/p material into the cutting tool, causing tool abrasion. This phenomenon results in increased Vb and a reduction in tool life. On the other hand, at a lower Vc of 45 m/min, the material removal rate is lower, resulting in reduced temperatures and less diffusion. Consequently, the Vb is comparatively less, leading to an extended tool life. The variation in Vb with distinct cooling settings is displayed in Fig. 3 . The N-MQL condition reduced the Vb in the range of 55–61 %, 35–47 %, and 19–22 % over dry, flood, and MQL cutting regimes, respectively. In the absence of any lubricant or coolant, dry cutting mains to higher friction and increased heat generation between the tool and the W/p, resulting in elevated Vb [29]. On the other hand, flood cooling effectively removes heat from the cutting region as a reason of continuous discharge of coolant, reducing the temperature and limiting tool wear. As a result, Vb is lower in flood cooling compared to dry cutting. MQL reduces friction and heat further by introducing a small amount of lubricant, resulting in even less Vb compared to flood cooling. N-MQL, utilizing CDs, provides superior lubrication and cooling effects, resulting in the least amount of Vb among the considered cutting environments. Higher Vc and dry cutting regime tend to increase Vb, while lower Vc and effective C/L environments reduce it.

Metal cutting is a complex process involving the removal of material from a W/p using a cutter. The mechanism of metal cutting is presented in Fig. 4 . The process can be explained through three main shear zones: primary shear zone (PSZ), secondary shear zone (SSZ), and tertiary shear zone (TSZ) [30]. Each cutting environment (dry, flood, MQL, and N-MQL) influences the heat transfer mechanisms within these zones differently. In the PSZ, a high-temperature region is formed due to the intense deformation and friction amid the cutting tool-W/p contact area. The heat generated is transferred mainly through conduction and, to a lesser extent, convection. The temperature in this zone can be estimated using the following equation (1) for conduction heat transfer:
(1)
Q
c o n d
= K × A ×
(
T
t o o l
−
T
W / p
)
/ t
where
Q
c o n d
is the heat transferred by conduction, k is the thermal conductivity of the W/p material, A is the area of the contact region amid the tool and the W/p,
T
t o o l
is the tool temperature,
T
W / p
is the W/p temperature, and t is the time of contact. In the SSZ, heat transfer occurs through both conduction and convection. The material experiences further plastic deformation, and chips are formed. The heat transfer rate increases due to the flow of coolant or lubricant under flood, MQL, and N-MQL environments. The heat transfer in this zone can be estimated using the equation for convective heat transfer:
(2)
Q
c o n v
= h × A ×
(
T
t o o l
−
T
c o o l a n t
)
where
Q
c o n v
is the heat transferred by convection, h is the convective heat transfer coefficient, A is the contact area between the tool and the coolant,
T
t o o l
is the tool temperature, and
T
c o o l a n t
is the coolant temperature. The TSZ is the region behind the cutting tool where the heat generated from the primary and secondary zones dissipates into the W/p. Heat transfer in this zone mainly occurs through conduction and can be approximated using the same formula as the PSZ. Adhesion wear occurs when material from the W/p transfers to the cutting tool due to localized welding and high temperatures. The wear rate due to adhesion can be described by the following formula (3):
(3)
V
a d h
=
K
a d h
× A × t
where
V
a d h
is the volume of material transferred due to adhesion,
K
a d h
is the adhesion wear coefficient, A is the contact area amid the tool and the W/p, and t is the time of contact. Abrasion wear happens when hard particles or asperities on the W/p surface plow into the softer material of the cutting tool, leading to material removal. The wear rate due to abrasion can be expressed using the following formula (4):
(4)
V
a b r
=
K
a b r
× F × d
where
V
a b r
is the volume of material removed due to abrasion,
K
a b r
is the abrasion wear coefficient, F is the normal force between the W/p and the tool, and d is the sliding distance or the length of the cut. In a dry-cutting environment, only conduction heat transfer is present, resulting in higher temperatures and increased tool wear due to the lack of C/L. In flood cooling, the convective heat transfer from the coolant reduces the PSZ and SSZ temperatures, improving tool life and surface finish. MQL introduces a small amount of lubricant, reducing heat generation and friction, and further improving the cutting process. N-MQL with CDs, enhances lubrication and cooling effects, leading to the most efficient heat dissipation and reduced temperatures in all shear zones. These variations in heat transfer mechanisms and temperature profiles have a noteworthy effect on cutter wear, surface quality, and cutting efficiency in the context of machining AMed specimens.

Fig. 5 (a–d) presents the tool wear (SEM images) and EDX images under distinct cutting regimes. Under both dry and flood cooling conditions, the observed wear mechanisms are abrasion and adhesion. These wear processes occur due to the direct contact amid the W/p material and the cutting tool, resulting in material removal from the tool and W/p e surfaces. In dry conditions, the absence of any cooling or lubricating medium exacerbates the friction and heat generated at the tool-W/p interface, leading to increased wear through abrasion and adhesion. The EDX analysis performed in the tooltip shows the presence of W/p elements. However, when considering MQL and N-MQL, coating peel-off is seen. MQL involves the controlled application of a minimal amount of lubricant to the cutting area, which serves to lessen friction and dissipate heat. In N-MQL, this lubricant includes NPs suspended in a base fluid, which can potentially enhance the lubrication and cooling effects.

In metal cutting processes, the effect of temperature plays a decisive role, and it is influenced by various factors such as Vc and the cutting environment. When cutting metals at distinct speeds, i.e., 45 m/min and 75 m/min, the temperature variation arises due to the conversion of mechanical energy into heat during the shearing of metal, which is displayed in Fig. 6 . At higher Vcs, more energy is transferred to the W/p, resulting in increased plastic deformation and friction amid the metal surface and the cutting tool. This leads to higher temperatures in the cutting zone. Conversely, lower Vcs generate comparatively lower frictional forces and deformation, leading to a reduced temperature rise. Furthermore, the cutting environment also significantly impacts the temperature variation. The N-MQL condition abridged the cutting heat in the range of 65–68 %, 43–48 %, and 26–29 % over dry, flood, and MQL regimes, respectively. In dry cutting, without any coolant or lubricant, heat dissipation is minimal, and higher temperatures can be observed due to increased friction and tool wear. On the other hand, flood cooling involves the continuous application of a coolant, which effectively reduces the temperature by carrying away the generated heat. MQL introduces a small amount of lubricant, reducing friction and resulting in lower temperatures compared to dry cutting. N-MQL takes this a step further by using CDs, offering even better C/L, thus reducing the temperature rise to the minimum. In summary, the temperature variation during metal cutting is influenced by the Vc and the type of cutting environment employed. Higher Vcs and dry cutting regimes lead to elevated temperatures due to increased friction and deformation, whereas lower speeds and the presence of efficient cooling methods, such as N-MQL, effectively dissipate heat, resulting in a more controlled and optimal cutting temperature. Understanding these temperature effects is crucial in selecting appropriate cutting parameters and cooling regimes to ensure improved cutting performance and prolonged tool life.

In metal cutting processes, the effect of Ra is significant and is influenced by Vc and the type of cutting environment employed [31]. When cutting metals at different speeds, such as 45 m/min and 75 m/min, Ra variation arises as a reason of the contact amid the W/p and the cutting tool. In metal cutting, higher Vcs generally lead to a smoother surface finish and a decrease in Ra, while lower Vcs tend to produce rougher surfaces with increased roughness. In the absence of any coolant or lubricant, the cutting tool and W/p experience higher friction and heat generation. The lack of effective cooling can lead to built-up edge formation, excessive tool wear, and increased Ra. The variation in Ra concerning distinct cooling environments is displayed in Fig. 7 . The N-MQL condition reduced the Ra in the range of 59–61 %, 36–38 %, and 18–24 % over dry, flood, and MQL regimes, respectively. Additionally, the absence of a lubricating medium may result in poor chip evacuation, causing irregularities on the W/p surface, and further contributing to higher Ra. In flood cooling, a continuous flow of coolant is applied during the cutting process. The coolant helps in reducing the cutting temperature, removing chips, and lubricating the tool-W/p interface. Effective C/L led to smoother chip formation, reduced friction, and less tool wear, resulting in a significant enhancement in the surface trait and a decrease in Ra. MQL introduces a small amount of lubricant as a mist or aerosol, which acts as both a coolant and lubricant. While it may not provide as much cooling as flood cooling, MQL still aids in reducing friction and preventing built-up edge formation. This leads to a smoother cutting process and a modest improvement in Ra compared to dry and flood cutting. N-MQL takes MQL a step further by using CDs, which provide superior L/C effects. The nano-lubricant particles can penetrate even smaller gaps in the tool-W/p interface, reducing friction and heat generation to a greater extent [32]. Consequently, this leads to finer chip formation, enhanced surface finish, and the lowest Ra among the considered cutting regimes.

Fig. 8 presented the 3D topography and SEM images under distinct cutting regimes. The dry condition presents a rough surface (high peaks and valleys) as a reason of more friction amid the tool-W/p. However, the N-MQL cutting regime presents low peaks and valleys as a reason of the C/L effect. The reason behind the fine surface was studied in the previous sections. The SEM images under the dry cutting regime show grooves, scratches, and smearing, but it is less in flood and MQL cutting regimes. Under the N-MQL cutting, no smearing or scratches were seen.

In metal cutting, the hardness of the machined face can be significantly influenced by Vc and the cutting environment. Fig. 9 displays the machined face hardness at a Vc of 75 m/min, distinct cutting environments exhibit distinct effects on hardness. In the dry cutting regime, the absence of coolant and lubricant leads to higher cutting temperatures, initiating a free convection heat transfer process. The elevated temperature in the cutting zone resulted in localized thermal softening of the material beneath the surface [33]. As a consequence, grain growth occurs, leading to a decrease in hardness. In flood cooling, the continuous application of coolant removes heat from the cutting zone, promoting forced convection heat transfer. The controlled cooling process helps to increase the hardness of the material compared to the no-coolant cutting regime. The efficient cooling prevents excessive thermal softening and minimizes grain growth, preserving the microstructure and hardness of the material. MQL and N-MQL provide even more effective C/L compared to flood cooling. The enhanced C/L properties limit the temperature rise during cutting, reducing thermal softening and grain growth. As a result, hardness at the machined surface was maintained at a higher level for N-MQL compared to dry, flood and MQL cooling.

The effect of microstructure and grain size is critical to the overall cutting performance and surface quality under distinct cutting environments, namely dry, flood, MQL, and N-MQL. The EBSD images under distinct cooling regimes are presented in Fig. 10 . AMed AISI 316L SS typically exhibits a fine-grained microstructure due to the rapid solidification during the AM process. However, during the cutting process, the cutting environment can influence the grain size further [34]. Under the N-MQL regime, where CDs are employed, the cutting environment experiences advanced lubrication and superior cooling effects. The nano-lubricant particles efficiently penetrate the fine-grain boundaries of the material and effectively reduce the temperature at the cutting zone. This controlled temperature decrease, significantly restricts the grain growth and recrystallization rate during the cutting process. Consequently, the grain size is substantially decreased under N-MQL regimes compared to other cutting environments, including dry cutting, flood cooling, and even MQL. The smaller grain size obtained through N-MQL has several advantageous effects. It contributes to a more refined microstructure on the machined surface, resulting in improved surface finish and reduced Ra. Moreover, the fine-grained microstructure enhances the mechanical properties of the machined AISI 316L SS, providing higher strength and hardness. Additionally, the reduced heat input and minimized grain coarsening under N-MQL regimes lead to decreased tool wear, prolonging the tool life and reducing the need for frequent tool replacements.

Formulated with diverse concentrations, the combination of CDs and RBO was meticulously prepared and characterized. Subsequent milling experiments were precisely conducted, encompassing 4 scenarios: N-MQL, MQL, conventional flood cooling, and dry machining. The overarching objective of this study revolved around enhancing the machined face of LPBF-manufactured 316L SS. Presented below are the salient findings that emerged from this investigation.
•
Cutting in a dry condition had the highest Vb in relation to other techniques. The Vb dropped when N-MQL was employed. When compared to a dry medium, N-MQL, MQL, and flood-cutting regimes reduce the Vb by 68%–76 %, 56%–55 %, and 47%–56 %, respectively. Excellent heat dissipation in the cutting zone considerably reduced friction and heat at the tool-W/p junction, resulting in rapid reductions in tool wear.
•
On the crater side of the tool, coating peel-off, abrasion, adhesion, and chipping were observed. The ability of the RBO + CDs to generate a C/L effect at the cutting region curtails the wear mechanisms on the tool and helps to extend tool life.
•
The cutting temperature was rising for the entire speed-feed combination under all cutting regimes. N-MQL reduced the temperature at the cutting region by providing a C/L effect.
•
Under N-MQL, the Ra was lesser for the entire speed-feed interactions. The Ra was decreased for dry, flood, and MQL regimes in contrast to the N-MQL regime to 59–61 %, 36–38 %, and 18–24 %, respectively. The 3D surface topography analysis indicated a smooth surface with fewer lows and peaks using the N-MQL cutting strategy. This is because N-MQL offers both L/C effects at the tool-W/p junction.
•
The performance of N-MQL directly affects the hardness on the machined side, as a reason for the higher level of C/L, which is reflected in the grain size refinement. The higher size of grain under dry regimes shows lower hardness due to no cooling.
Future Recommendations
Below are some suggestions for further research:
•
Some kind of analytical model may be created to predict tool wear rates, particularly under diverse cooling situations.
•
In upcoming studies, the effects of various nanofluids with distinct concentrations will be examined concerning tool wear outcomes.
•
Statistical studies concerning the machining characteristics could be calculated in the future to obtain optimal conditions.
The research leading to these results has received funding from the Norway Grants 2014–2021 operated by National Science Centre under Project Contract No 2020/37/K/ST8/02795.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
